DOOM E1M1 — real id geometry, rendered by p‑bits

Not an arena — the actual E1M1 "Hangar" map from doom1.wad (467 vertexes, 475 linedefs, 85 sectors). Each screen column is cast as a ray against the real linedefs and walked front‑to‑back through a clip window, so real per‑sector floor/ceiling heights, steps and windows appear. That framebuffer is the bias field of a Potts lattice; chromatic block‑Gibbs relaxes into the image. Click the screen and walk id's real level.
sampler verified vs THRML

H = data — + coupling — = —
xᵢ ← argmaxₖ [ β·rewardₖ + Gumbel ]  ·  rewardₖ = −bias(k) + J·Σⱼ[xⱼ=k]  ·  J=0.32, K=64 (Potts, categorical)
probe …

      
Every number is read straight out of the running sampler each frame — not invented UI metrics. Drop β and watch state transitions explode and energy climb as the lattice boils; raise it and it descends back. Hit Show changed cells to see exactly which p‑bits the sampler is still moving. This Gibbs conditional is the one verified against Extropic’s THRML in tsu_engine.py (Δenergy 0.00).

Developer / proof mode: Show bias field = the raycast target the
        p‑bits relax toward; changed cells = the p‑bits the sampler
        moved last sweep; Freeze + Step to advance one Gibbs sweep at a time; Inspect
        then click any pixel to read its live conditional P(k). Drop β→0 and the world dissolves
        into pure categorical noise; drop βvis and even the geometry boils
        — which wall you see is sampled too. A static image can't do either.

    

  


  

    
—

    
The browser is not displaying a precomputed image. It is displaying the
      instantaneous state of a stochastic computation. Don't trust that — verify it: drop
      β to 0 and watch the world evaporate into pure categorical noise; Freeze the sampler
      and Step one sweep at a time; Inspect any pixel to read its live conditional P(k).

    
            INPUT (keys)  — clamp
                 |
     real DOOM E1M1 geometry  (467 verts, 475 linedefs, 85 sectors)  — the fixed problem
                 |   per-column VISIBILITY is SAMPLED:  wall ~ argmaxₖ[-βᵛᵢₓ·depth + Gumbel]
          constraint / bias field   (crisp raycast = βᵛᵢₓ→∞ limit)
                 |
      K=64 Potts lattice   H = -bias(k) + J*Σ[x₁≠x₂]
                 |   chromatic block-Gibbs relaxation  (Gumbel-max, even/odd blocks)
        current p-bit state  (25,000 cells)
                 |
           browser framebuffer   ← you are here

    
How each part maps to the sampler (precise, not hand‑wavy):

      ✓ SAMPLED p‑bits — every displayed pixel: framebuffer, id textures, per‑sector lighting, denoising, particles.

      ✓ SAMPLED decisions: NPC intelligence & pathing; and the visible surface itself — which wall each column sees is a Gumbel‑max sample over depth (raycast‑as‑inference); the exact raycast is its βvis→∞ limit. Drop βvis and the geometry boils.

      ✓ ENERGY terms & constraints: walls & doors = cut couplings (a closed door blocks colour diffusion); player collision = projecting position out of hard wall constraints.

      × CLAMPS (deterministic, correctly so): keypresses; and id's linedef geometry itself — the fixed problem definition the energies are built from.

      × not yet: audio.

    
The sampler implementation is verified against the THRML reference in
      tsu_engine.py (Δenergy 0.00) — behavioural equivalence in simulation.
      This is stochastic relaxation via Gibbs sampling over a Hamiltonian — the same class of
      probabilistic computation explored in p‑bit / thermodynamic‑computing research (IBM, Extropic,
      Normal Computing); this browser build is a software model of that execution style, not the
      silicon. On a CPU the sampler is a library routine; here the whole computation is the sampler.
      SCIN / ThermoSoft.
